title: Ezekiel 32
subtitle: Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Oracle Against Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1-16 — The Sea Monster Caught in a Net

heb.paragraph: The qinah is a formal funeral dirge, characterized by a distinctive limping meter (3+2 stressed syllables). Ezekiel 'raises a qinah' over Pharaoh—composing a funeral song for a king who is not yet dead. This prophetic anticipation of death is a powerful rhetorical device: by singing the funeral, Ezekiel makes the death certain. The lament form simultaneously mourns and pronounces judgment, creating an unsettling emotional duality.

The kherem is a large fishing or hunting net. God will cast his kherem over Pharaoh-as-monster (v. 3), recalling the tannin imagery of chapter 29 but now completing the action: the monster is caught, dragged out, and thrown onto the open field. The word also carries overtones of the 'ban' (kherem) in holy war contexts, suggesting that Pharaoh's capture is an act of sacred destruction.

refs.note: The tannin imagery from chapter 29 is reprised and completed. There Pharaoh was compared to a crocodile in the Nile; here the monster is caught, butchered, and spread across the landscape.
The cosmic darkening of sun, moon, and stars in Ezekiel 32:7-8 anticipates the apocalyptic imagery of Revelation's sixth seal, where heavenly bodies respond to divine judgment.
Jesus draws on the same prophetic tradition when he describes the sun being darkened and stars falling before the Son of Man's coming.

notes.note: Dated March 585 BC. Pharaoh thinks of himself as a lion, but God calls him a monster (tannin) thrashing in the waters. Self-perception and divine assessment rarely align for the proud.

God casts his net with 'a company of many peoples'—the Babylonian coalition. The monster is hauled onto land and left for birds and beasts, a fate worse than death in ancient culture where proper burial was essential.

The cosmic darkening—covering the sun, moon, and stars—is apocalyptic language signaling that Egypt's fall has universal significance. This is Day-of-the-LORD imagery applied to a specific historical event.
Nations will be horrified and kings will shudder at Pharaoh's fall. The psychological impact on the ancient world mirrors the shock that followed the actual decline of Egyptian power.
This is identified as a qinah that the 'daughters of the nations' will chant. Professional mourning women performed funeral laments; here the nations themselves mourn Egypt.
Calvin observes the gap between Pharaoh's self-image (a lion) and God's assessment (a thrashing monster). This is the universal condition of the proud: they see majesty where God sees chaos.

Calvin interprets the cosmic darkening literally and figuratively: Egypt's fall dims the light of the political world, but it also foreshadows the final judgment when all heavenly lights will be extinguished.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew distinguishes between 'lion of the nations' (kephir goyim, Pharaoh's self-image) and 'monster in the seas' (tannin ba-yammim, God's characterization). The contrast is deliberate and deflating.

The cosmic imagery of covering the sun and darkening the stars draws on ancient Near Eastern theophanic traditions. Such language was conventional for describing the fall of great powers (cf. Isa 13:10).

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block classifies this as a 'lament-become-judgment' oracle, where the funeral form is co-opted for prophetic purposes. The tannin imagery creates a literary inclusio with chapter 29. Block notes the escalation: in ch. 29 the monster is hooked; here it is netted, butchered, and scattered. The cosmic darkening (vv. 7-8) elevates the oracle to mythological proportions, and the daughters-of-the-nations refrain (v. 16) gives it liturgical weight.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli highlights the tension between the qinah form (which normally expresses genuine grief) and the triumphant content (which celebrates the enemy's defeat). This 'ironic lament' is a prophetic innovation that serves theological purposes: mourning the loss of a once-great creature that God himself had made beautiful (cf. 31:9), while simultaneously celebrating YHWH's victory over chaos.

hist.context: Dated March 585 BC (v. 1), this lament arrives after Jerusalem's fall (586 BC), which Ezekiel learned about in 33:21. The first half (vv. 1-16) returns to the sea-monster imagery of chapter 29 but transforms it into a funeral dirge. Pharaoh considered himself a lion among nations but is actually a thrashing monster in the waters. God will haul him out with a net and scatter his flesh across mountains and valleys—cosmic in scope, as even celestial bodies are darkened (vv. 7-8).

sections.header: Verses 17-32 — The Descent to the Pit Among the Uncircumcised

heb.paragraph: The bor (pit) is synonymous with Sheol but carries connotations of a cistern or dungeon—a place of confinement rather than simply rest. The 'pit' in Ezekiel's afterlife geography is stratified: the uncircumcised and those who fell by the sword occupy the lowest regions, while the ancient warriors who 'went down to Sheol with their weapons of war' lie in a slightly different zone (v. 27). This is the most elaborate mapping of the underworld in the Hebrew Bible.

The ʿarelim (uncircumcised) appears as a term of shame and exclusion throughout this descent poem. Being among the uncircumcised in death is the ultimate disgrace for Egypt, which practiced circumcision. The repeated refrain creates a catalog of fallen empires—Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom, Sidon—all consigned to the same shameful zone. The theological implication is clear: military might, cultural achievement, and imperial glory are all leveled in death.

refs.note: Isaiah's descent-to-Sheol poem for Babylon's king is the closest parallel: the dead rise to greet the newcomer with mocking surprise. Both texts develop the underworld as a space of ironic leveling.

Paul declares that at the name of Jesus every knee will bow, 'in heaven and on earth and under the earth.' Ezekiel's three-tiered cosmos (heavens, earth, underworld) provides part of the background for this confession.

notes.note: This second oracle is dated two weeks after the first (April 585 BC). It conducts a guided tour of the underworld, showing Pharaoh where he will lie.

Assyria is there, surrounded by the graves of its slain. Once the terror of the living, now powerless in the pit. This would have been vivid for Ezekiel's audience, who remembered Assyria's fall in 612 BC.
Elam, the ancient power east of Babylon, also lies among the uncircumcised. Its warriors who 'spread terror in the land of the living' now bear their shame.
Meshech and Tubal (regions in Asia Minor) are there, along with the ancient warriors who went to Sheol with their weapons. Unlike these honored dead, the nations that 'spread terror' lie in disgrace.

The chapter concludes devastatingly: Pharaoh will see these fallen nations and be 'consoled'—the grim comfort of shared misery. God declares: 'I spread my terror in the land of the living'—true terror belongs to God alone.

Calvin emphasizes that even the 'mighty leaders' in Sheol speak to Pharaoh from among the dead. No earthly authority survives death. The voice of the dead warriors mocking new arrivals is a powerful image of the futility of worldly power.

Calvin contrasts the honored dead warriors (buried with their weapons) with the dishonored fallen nations (who bear their shame). Even in death, there are degrees of dignity, determined by whether one's violence was within God's purposes or against them.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The command to 'wail' (neheh) for Egypt's hordes and 'consign them' to the underworld implies that Ezekiel's prophetic speech itself has performative power—the lament enacts what it describes.

The reference to warriors who 'went down to Sheol with their weapons of war, their swords placed under their heads' reflects ancient burial practices where weapons were interred with warriors, a custom attested archaeologically across the Near East.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block calls this the 'grand tour of the underworld,' the most developed afterlife topography in the Hebrew Bible. The catalog of nations (Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom, Sidon) follows a rough geographic pattern and represents the full sweep of ancient Near Eastern imperial history. Block emphasizes the repeated refrain—'they bear their shame with those who go down to the pit'—as a liturgical element that gives the poem a dirge-like quality. The final irony: Pharaoh will be 'consoled' by seeing that everyone else is dead too.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies this passage as drawing on ancient traditions of the netherworld visit (katabasis), known in both Mesopotamian (Descent of Ishtar) and Greek (Odyssey XI) literature. However, Ezekiel's version is uniquely theological: the underworld tour serves not as adventure narrative but as a comprehensive statement about the leveling power of divine judgment. Zimmerli highlights the priestly concern with circumcision as a boundary marker—even in death, the uncircumcised remain outsiders.

hist.context: Dated April 585 BC (two weeks after vv. 1-16), this descent-to-the-pit poem is the most elaborate afterlife vision in the Hebrew Bible. Pharaoh is escorted down to the underworld and shown the nations already there: Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom, the Sidonians, and their princes. Each nation 'bears its shame' among the uncircumcised. The catalog functions as a roll call of fallen empires, reminding Pharaoh that he joins a long queue of once-mighty but now powerless rulers.

themes.note: Judgment and prophecy dominate as Ezekiel composes the most elaborate descent-to-the-underworld passage in the Hebrew Bible. Sovereignty is absolute: God commands even the geography of Sheol.

lit.note: Two complementary poems: the first deals Pharaoh's death (lament for the monster), the second his afterlife (guided tour of Sheol). Together they complete the Egypt oracle cycle begun in chapter 29.

Being among the uncircumcised in death is the ultimate disgrace for Egypt, which practiced circumcision. The repeated refrain creates a catalog of fallen empires—Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom, Sidon—all consigned to the same shameful zone. The theological implication is clear: military might, cultural achievement, and imperial glory are all leveled in death.

ppl.text: 1), this lament arrives after Jerusalem's fall (586 BC), which Ezekiel learned about in 33:21…

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: Pharaoh considered himself a lion among nations but is actually a thrashing monster in the waters…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit

translations.text: I will throw you on the land and hurl you on the open field. I will let all the birds of the sky settle on you and all the animals of the wild gorge themselves on you.
And I will cast you on the ground; on the open field I will fling you, and will cause all the birds of the heavens to settle on you, and I will gorge the beasts of the whole earth with you.
I will drench the land with your flowing blood all the way to the mountains, and the ravines will be filled with your flesh.
I will drench the land even to the mountains with your flowing blood, and the ravines will be full of you.

thread.anchor: Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit
thread.text: The tannin imagery from chapter 29 is reprised and completed. There Pharaoh was compared to a crocodile in the Nile; here the monster is caught, butchered, and spread across the landscape.

thread.anchor: Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit
thread.text: The cosmic darkening of sun, moon, and stars in Ezekiel 32:7-8 anticipates the apocalyptic imagery of Revelation's sixth seal, where heavenly bodies respond to divine judgment.

thread.anchor: Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit
thread.text: Jesus draws on the same prophetic tradition when he describes the sun being darkened and stars falling before the Son of Man's coming.

thread.anchor: Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit
thread.text: Isaiah's descent-to-Sheol poem for Babylon's king is the closest parallel: the dead rise to greet the newcomer with mocking surprise. Both texts develop the underworld as a space of ironic leveling.

thread.anchor: Lament for Pharaoh and Descent to the Pit
thread.text: Paul declares that at the name of Jesus every knee will bow, 'in heaven and on earth and under the earth.' Ezekiel's three-tiered cosmos (heavens, earth, underworld) provides part of the background for this confession.

coaching.tip: Pharaoh thinks he's a lion but is actually a 'monster in the seas' (v. 2) — caught in God's net, dragged onto land, left for birds and beasts. Self-perception doesn't match reality. Ezekiel consistently strips rulers of their self-image and replaces it with God's assessment. The gap between how empires see themselves and how God sees them is the prophet's recurring subject.

The descent-to-Sheol catalogue (vv. 17–32) is the most elaborate underworld scene in the Old Testament. Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom, Sidon — each lies in its own section of the pit, 'bearing their shame.' Ezekiel maps Sheol like a city with districts. The literary effect is cumulative: every empire that terrorized the living world now occupies a silent ward. Egypt is about to join them. The roll call of the dead is the prophet's strongest argument against imperial confidence.
